parentQuestion: A patient with a brainstem stroke presents with bilateral loss of pain and temperature sensation over the entire body with preserved fine touch and proprioception. Where is the lesion located?

scaffolds.q: Pain/temperature and fine touch/proprioception travel in SEPARATE pathways.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Pain/temp travels in the spinothalamic tract. Fine touch/proprioception travels in the dorsal columns → medial lemniscus. Different tracts!

scaffolds.q: The spinothalamic tract carries pain and temperature sensation.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The spinothalamic tract (anterolateral system) is the dedicated pathway for pain and temperature from the body.

scaffolds.q: The medial lemniscus carries fine touch, vibration, and proprioception.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Dorsal column fibers synapse in the medulla, cross, and form the medial lemniscus - carrying discriminative touch and position sense.

scaffolds.q: These two pathways run through different parts of the medulla.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! In the medulla, the spinothalamic tracts run more centrally/medially, while the medial lemniscus runs more laterally/ventrally.

scaffolds.q: A small central medullary lesion could damage spinothalamic tracts bilaterally while sparing the medial lemnisci.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Because of their different positions, a strategically placed central lesion can selectively damage one tract type while sparing the other.

scaffolds.q: Bilateral loss of pain/temp with preserved fine touch means the spinothalamic tracts are damaged but the medial lemnisci are intact.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The pattern of selective sensory loss tells you which tracts are damaged. The dissociation is the key to localization.

scaffolds.q: This dissociation (pain/temp lost, fine touch preserved) can only happen where the tracts are anatomically separated.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! In the thalamus, all sensory info converges. But in the medulla, the tracts are still separate - a lesion can hit one and spare the other.

scaffolds.q: Bilateral pain/temp loss with preserved fine touch = central medulla lesion.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The central medulla contains both spinothalamic tracts close together. A small central lesion damages both while sparing the more lateral medial lemnisci.

scaffolds.q: The answer is CENTRAL MEDULLA, BILATERAL - where spinothalamic tracts run before the medial lemnisci.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Not lateral medulla (that's Wallenberg with crossed sensory loss), not dorsal columns (that would cause the opposite - loss of fine touch), not thalamus (all modalities would be affected). CENTRAL MEDULLA explains this dissociation.
